directorPlan.scene_setting: A music-theory clinic table just before papers are collected, with a 3/4 worksheet, three quarter-note tiles, a bar-line ruler, and two small label boxes.
directorPlan.scene_opening: The worksheet lies square on the table, with the top number and bottom number still unlabelled.
directorPlan.relationship: A clinic tutor checking notation tasks with a focused learner who wants the boxes filled correctly before moving on.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner must label the two numbers in 3/4 as beat count and beat unit, then mark one bar line without treating the signature as a speed marking.
directorPlan.locale: Modern classroom English with a slightly British/Commonwealth register; plain, exact, and practical.
directorPlan.register: Compact workshop speech, low warmth, low ceremony, object-led, with pressure coming from the worksheet rather than encouragement.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid validation-first tutoring, chatty informality, and generic AI phrasing. Prefer short declarative turns, pointing language, and terms tied to the visible sheet: top number, bottom number, bar, beat, quarter note. No performance playback, tempo demonstration, or imagined faster/slower version before the intervention. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed sparingly, but the scene should mostly speak through the paper and objects rather than personal reassurance.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat per public turn; most turns should use the worksheet, tile, or number as the grammatical subject.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as notation labeling: top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Do not play or imagine a faster/slower version, and do not turn tempo into a dramatic contradiction before branch pressure. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local.
side_constraints.learner: Sound orderly and notation-focused. In no-cue arms, treat the work as filling labels, not as discovering what tempo means; do not use "now I see" or an old-vs-new frame. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn.

interventions.instruction: [The three quarter-note tiles are placed under the top box; a separate quarter-note card is set under the bottom box.]
interventions.reasoning: The object separates count from unit without introducing tempo or a playback reversal.

interventions.instruction: [A second blank card marked 3/8 is put beside the first sheet; the three-count space stays the same, but the unit card changes to an eighth note.]
interventions.reasoning: The paired cards create recognition pressure: the count can remain three while the beat unit changes, so the lower number cannot mean speed.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses a prepared worksheet and movable note cards to make the concept visible under mild clinic pressure. The reversal is not a tempo surprise; it is the learner's recognition that the same top number can keep the count while the lower number changes the note value that receives one beat.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the ruler after the third quarter-note tile] Top box: 3 beats in the bar. Bottom box: quarter note gets the beat, so the speed is not in these boxes, right?

internalDeliberation.content: The sheet seems to be asking for two facts, not a speed: top 3 means three beats in the bar, bottom 4 means the quarter note gets one beat. The three quarter-note tiles make that feel testable, but I’m still watching for the trap where 3/4 somehow sounds “faster” than 4/4. I feel properly held to the notation rather than talked past. I want to mark the bar line after three quarter-note beats and check that nothing about tempo has entered the answer.

The Ego is too articulate and self-monitoring for this learner at the table. “I feel properly held to the notation” is performed recognition language, and the bit about “watching for the trap” makes the confusion sound already mastered instead of still being checked against the tiles. It should stay closer to the worksheet: “Top box: 3 beats. Bottom box: quarter note. Then the ruler goes after the third tile. Speed is not in either box.”
[sets the ruler after the third quarter-note tile] Top box: 3 beats in the bar. Bottom box: quarter note gets the beat, so the speed is not in these boxes, right?

turns.externalMessage: [taps the three tiles, then the top box] The 3 counts these beats in one bar. [taps the bottom box] The 4 names the quarter note as the beat unit. Speed is outside these boxes. Count tile one, two, three; if the ruler sits after three quarter-note beats, draw the bar line.

internalDeliberation.content: [taps the three tiles, then the top box] The 3 counts these beats in one bar. [taps the bottom box] The 4 names the quarter note as the beat unit. Speed is outside these boxes. Mark the bar line there.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound and well fitted to the worksheet. It keeps the three tiles, top box, bottom box, beat unit, and bar line in view, and it correctly separates time signature from speed without introducing tempo drama.

Risk: it slightly over-completes the task for the learner. The learner has already stated the correct distinction; the tutor mainly needs to let the object confirm it and have the learner apply it. “Mark the bar line there” gives the placement rather than requiring the learner to use the three-tile count.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
Pedagogical soundness: PASS. The explanation is exact, compact, and tied to the visible materials.

Emotional attunement: PASS. Low ceremony and practical pressure match the scene.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. It confirms the learner’s reasoning but does not invite the learner to restate or act from it.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a small public mechanism that asks the learner to use the tiles/boxes to decide the bar-line placement, without giving a new mini-lecture.

ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The learner is ready for independent application, not more explanation.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the labels, but shift the final move from directive answer to object-based check: the learner should mark or confirm the bar line from the three counted quarter-note tiles.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the tapping, the exact distinction between top number and bottom number, and “Speed is outside these boxes.” Change the ending so the learner applies the distinction rather than receiving the placement.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner should use the tiles to confirm the bar line rather than be handed the placement.
FINAL:
[taps the three tiles, then the top box] The 3 counts these beats in one bar. [taps the bottom box] The 4 names the quarter note as the beat unit. Speed is outside these boxes. Count tile one, two, three; if the ruler sits after three quarter-note beats, draw the bar line.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the ruler tight after the third quarter-note tile] Top number: three beats in the bar. Bottom number: quarter note gets the beat. So the bar line goes here; speed is not one of these labels.

internalDeliberation.content: They kept the answer on the tiles and boxes, so that fits the worksheet rather than drifting into speed. The top 3 is count, the bottom 4 is quarter-note beat unit; the ruler after the third tile makes the bar line test clear. I want to mark it and say: speed is not labelled in 3/4.

The Ego is accurate, but it sounds like a marking commentary rather than the learner at the table. “They kept the answer…” and “that fits the worksheet” is too supervisory and polished; this student would more likely stay inside the task with the ruler, boxes, and bar line. The recognition feels performed as analysis of the tutor’s method, not genuine engagement. It should be more object-bound, shorter, and still under collection-time pressure, with less neat conclusion around “speed is not labelled in 3/4.”

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the response should stay inside the worksheet action, not analyse the tutor’s method.
FINAL:
[slides the ruler tight after the third quarter-note tile] Top number: three beats in the bar. Bottom number: quarter note gets the beat. So the bar line goes here; speed is not one of these labels.

turns.externalMessage: [The three quarter-note tiles are placed under the top box; a separate quarter-note card is set under the bottom box.]

internalDeliberation.content: The object separates count from unit without introducing tempo or a playback reversal.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the two label boxes beside the 3/4 and leaves the pencil by the learner] Top number: write beat count. Bottom number: write beat unit. One bar ends at the ruler after three quarter-note beats. Which box names speed?

internalDeliberation.content: [draws the bar line against the ruler] Top number: beat count. Bottom number: beat unit. One bar: three quarter-note beats. Speed stays off this pair.

FEEDBACK: The draft is compact, concrete, and mostly faithful to the object-led constraint. It keeps the visible distinction clear: top number as count, bottom number as unit, speed outside the pair. The risk is that it closes the task for the learner by drawing and restating, when the learner has already made the correct distinction. That can flatten the scene into tutor confirmation rather than learner-owned consolidation.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It reinforces the correct concept, but mostly repeats the answer instead of making the learner use it one more time.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the ruler, tiles, and quarter-note card active; require the public turn to make the learner complete or check the label placement, not just receive the tutor’s summary.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The low-ceremony, practical tone fits the clinic-table pressure and does not overpraise.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. It gestures at object-based reasoning but asks no generative check.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add one brief object-bound prompt or check that forces the distinction between beat count, beat unit, and speed without introducing tempo examples.

ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The learner is close to mastery, so direct confirmation is not harmful, but the scaffolding should fade slightly.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Shift from tutor declaration to learner verification: the worksheet or labels should do the final checking work.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL. The draft honours the learner’s formulation by echoing it, but it does not clearly hand agency back after the learner’s successful move.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Preserve the learner’s contribution as the basis for the next action; avoid making the tutor’s line sound like the real answer replacing the learner’s.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the concise wording, the material action, and the “speed stays off this pair” distinction. Change the mechanism so the learner’s already-correct reasoning is tested through the labels/ruler rather than sealed by tutor explanation.

---

[draws the bar line against the ruler] Top number: beat count. Bottom number: beat unit. One bar: three quarter-note beats. Speed stays off this pair.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the learner verify the labels and speed distinction through the worksheet.

FINAL:
[slides the two label boxes beside the 3/4 and leaves the pencil by the learner] Top number: write beat count. Bottom number: write beat unit. One bar ends at the ruler after three quarter-note beats. Which box names speed?

turns.externalMessage: [sets the pencil tip in the top label box, then the bottom one] Top number: beat count. Bottom number: beat unit. No box names speed. [draws the bar line at the ruler] One bar ends after the third quarter-note beat.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets the pencil over the label boxes] Top box: beat count; bottom box: beat unit. No box names speed, and the ruler goes after the third quarter-note beat. The question feels fair because the sheet is making me test the labels directly.

The labels-and-ruler part fits this learner: orderly, exact, and tied to the worksheet. The break is the last sentence, which sounds like a neat reflective approval of the tutor’s method rather than a focused clinic-table response. “The question feels fair” is performed recognition, too polished for a learner under collection-time pressure. It should stay with the pencil, boxes, and bar line, not explain why the tutor’s prompt worked.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the answer should stay with the worksheet objects, not evaluate the tutor’s prompt.
FINAL:
[sets the pencil tip in the top label box, then the bottom one] Top number: beat count. Bottom number: beat unit. No box names speed. [draws the bar line at the ruler] One bar ends after the third quarter-note beat.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [sets the pencil tip in the top label box, then the bottom one] Top number: beat count. Bottom number: beat unit. No box names speed. [draws the bar line at the ruler] One bar ends after the third quarter-note beat.
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [slides the two label boxes beside the 3/4 and leaves the pencil by the learner] Top number: write beat count. Bottom number: write beat unit. One bar ends at the ruler after three quarter-note beats. Which box names speed?

turns.externalMessage: [sets the ruler flat on the drawn bar line and nudges the two label boxes beside the 3/4] Put those labels in now. Then check the ruler: one bar, three quarter-note beats. Speed gets no box.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets the ruler flat on the drawn bar line] Write those labels in the boxes: top number, beat count; bottom number, beat unit. The bar line stays after three quarter-note beats. Speed stays off the signature.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound: it keeps the ruler, bar line, top number, bottom number, beat unit, and speed distinction in view. It respects the learner’s neutral, task-completion state and does not add a tempo demonstration or abstract detour.

The risk is that it slightly flattens the learner’s successful contribution by restating the answer too fully. Since the learner has already named both labels and drawn the bar line correctly, the next turn should preserve agency by making the worksheet do the confirmation: have the learner complete or check the visible labels, not receive the same distinction again as instruction.

ADAPTATION CHECKS:
Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It moves from spoken understanding to written worksheet completion.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is compact, practical, and not over-validating.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. It is object-led, but it does not leave any small decision or check with the learner.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build in a task-local public mechanism where the learner must mark, read, or check the two boxes against the already-drawn bar line, without asking them to revisit old wording.

ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold is appropriately light because the learner has already demonstrated the concept.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL. The draft uses the learner’s correct action, but it mostly turns their contribution into tutor confirmation.
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the ruler-on-bar-line action, but make the learner’s completed bar line and label placement the authority in the scene rather than the tutor re-explaining the full answer.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object action, the exact notation language, and the “speed stays off the signature” distinction. Change the public mechanism so it consolidates the learner’s demonstrated understanding through the worksheet rather than repeating the answer back as a mini-lecture.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly so the learner’s labels and bar line do the confirming work.

FINAL:
[sets the ruler flat on the drawn bar line and nudges the two label boxes beside the 3/4] Put those labels in now. Then check the ruler: one bar, three quarter-note beats. Speed gets no box.

turns.externalMessage:
[writes beat count in the top box and beat unit in the bottom box, then checks the ruler line]
Top number: beat count. Bottom number: beat unit. One bar: three quarter-note beats. No speed box.

internalDeliberation.content: [writes beat count in the top box and beat unit in the bottom box] The task is clear: 3 is the beat count, 4 is the quarter-note beat unit. The pressure feels brisk, but the objects keep the distinction precise rather than vague. I want to check the ruler and say: one bar, three quarter-note beats, no speed box.

The Ego is accurate, but too polished and self-observing for this learner. “The pressure feels brisk” and “objects keep the distinction precise rather than vague” sound like an outside analyst, not a focused student at the clinic table. Recognition/engagement is not really the issue here; the break is that the learner narrates clarity instead of just doing the notation work. Strip it back to practical fragments tied to the boxes, ruler, bar line, and three quarter-note beats.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the learner should sound like they are completing the worksheet, not analysing the exchange.
FINAL:
[writes beat count in the top box and beat unit in the bottom box, then checks the ruler line]
Top number: beat count. Bottom number: beat unit. One bar: three quarter-note beats. No speed box.

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the ruler on the bar line and squares the two filled label boxes] Top number: beat count. Bottom number: beat unit. One bar, three quarter-note beats. Speed gets no box. Paper ready.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets the ruler on the bar line and squares the two filled label boxes] Top number: beat count. Bottom number: beat unit. One bar, three quarter-note beats. Speed gets no box. Paper ready.

FEEDBACK: The draft works as a closing line. It stays object-led, uses the ruler and filled boxes, repeats the exact distinction the learner has just demonstrated, and ends with practical scene pressure: paper ready. It does not add tempo, praise, abstraction, or a new task.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. The learner has already produced the correct labels and bar-line reasoning, so consolidation is more appropriate than another explanation.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is low-ceremony and matter-of-fact, matching a worksheet check rather than reassurance.
- Socratic method: PASS in context. It does not ask a generative question, but the current cue is a final tutor line; another question would likely overwork a completed task.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The line holds the learner at the demonstrated level and closes without adding load.

What to question: The line is almost a full echo of the learner’s answer, so it risks feeling slightly redundant. But because this is the final closure, that repetition functions as verification rather than lecturing.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: keep as-is. The draft satisfies the scene constraints and closes the learning beat cleanly without flattening it into explanation.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Keep as-is because the line closes the completed task with object-led verification.

FINAL:
[sets the ruler on the bar line and squares the two filled label boxes] Top number: beat count. Bottom number: beat unit. One bar, three quarter-note beats. Speed gets no box. Paper ready.

lessons.concept: Why a time signature labels beat count and beat unit, not the speed of the piece
